content.text: Every long-running agentic session eventually confronts a hard limit: the model's context window. Before a new API call is made the client must decide which messages to keep, which to compress, and which to discard entirely—a decision that profoundly affects whether the agent remains coherent across dozens or hundreds of turns. Truncation strategies are the algorithms that make this decision systematically, balancing information retention against token budget.

A truncation strategy is a client-side algorithm that selects which messages (or parts of messages) to include in the next API request when the accumulated conversation history would exceed the model's context-window limit. The algorithm runs before the HTTP call and produces a reduced message list that fits within the budget while preserving as much decision-relevant information as possible.

—the ordered list of system, user, assistant, tool_call, and tool_result turns that form a conversation. It differs from context compression (which rewrites content to be shorter) and from retrieval-augmented generation (which fetches external knowledge). Truncation is purely about

The problem is not hypothetical. Models with a 128 k-token window fill up in roughly 30–60 multi-step agent turns once tool results and retrieved documents are included. Without a truncation strategy, the agent crashes with a 'context length exceeded' API error, losing all in-flight state.

Truncation is as architecturally important as the system prompt itself. A poorly designed strategy silently degrades agent behaviour long before it causes an outright error. Consider three failure modes:
— dropping the early user turn that described the task objective leaves the agent working towards an implicit, reconstructed goal. It keeps running but solves the wrong problem.
— if a tool_call message is kept but the corresponding tool_result is dropped (or vice-versa), most model APIs return a validation error. The agent crashes mid-task.
— waiting until the API returns a 'context length exceeded' error means the call has already failed and the response is lost. Recovery requires reconstructing state from logs.

Good truncation avoids all three. It also controls cost: a 200 k-token prompt can cost 10–20× more than an equivalent 20 k-token prompt on input-token-priced APIs. Keeping the context lean directly reduces per-turn expenditure.
— agents run for hours without crashing, because the context is managed proactively.
— fewer input tokens per call; important when models charge $3–15 per million input tokens.
— a consistent truncation policy produces reproducible context shapes, making debugging easier than ad-hoc size checks.
— truncation can be layered with summarisation and retrieval. The truncation layer decides what to drop; a summarisation layer decides what to compress; a retrieval layer decides what to add back.
— by pinning the system prompt, safety instructions and persona directives remain visible regardless of conversation length.
No truncation strategy is free of cost. Each removes information that could—in some future turn—be relevant to the model's reasoning. The key trade-offs are:
— once a message is dropped it is gone unless an external store backs it up. Early user instructions or crucial tool results can be silently erased.
— generating a summary requires an extra LLM call (typically 200–600 ms). This increases end-to-end latency for the turn that triggers compaction.
— the summarising model can misrepresent details, especially numeric values, entity names, and dates. A wrong summary propagates silently through subsequent turns.
— hybrid strategies (summarise + retrieve + prune) require more state management. Each layer introduces failure modes that must be monitored in production.
— keeping non-contiguous message ranges can confuse models that rely on conversational continuity. A message referencing 'the file you sent earlier' breaks if that earlier message was pruned.
Before each API call the orchestrator computes the total token count of the candidate message list and compares it against the available budget. The budget is:
is the model's context-window size and
is the token budget reserved for the model's output (the
parameter). If the current message list exceeds

The simplest strategy removes messages from the oldest end of the history until the list fits. The system prompt is always anchored at position 0 and is never dropped. Atomic pairs (tool_call + tool_result) are removed together to avoid orphaning.

If the list contains N messages the worst-case number of removal steps is O(N). In practice, because messages are removed in pairs, it converges in O(N/2) steps. FIFO is cheap but produces permanent information loss.

A sliding summarisation window collects the oldest k messages, calls the LLM to produce a brief summary, injects the summary as a system or user message, and discards the originals. This preserves semantic content at a 10–20× compression ratio while introducing one extra LLM call per compaction.
Typical trigger: when utilisation crosses 70% of the available budget. Waiting until 95% leaves insufficient headroom for the summary text itself.

Keep only the most recent k message-pairs (user + assistant) plus the pinned system prompt and the latest user turn. This is O(1) per call once k is fixed. It is effective when recent context is all that matters—for instance, a coding assistant where each task is largely independent.

Each message is embedded into a vector. Before each API call the orchestrator embeds the latest user query and retrieves the top-k most semantically similar historical messages. The resulting context is non-contiguous: the system prompt, top-k retrieved turns, and the most recent window are stitched together in chronological order.

Tool results (search hits, database rows, code-execution output) are often the largest items in the context. Before general truncation, a pruning pass removes or truncates verbose fields within tool results: raw HTML from web searches, full stack traces, padded log lines. Structured numeric or entity data is preserved; prose is compressed or dropped.

The diagram below shows the truncation pipeline. The full history buffer (left) accumulates every turn. When the token budget is exceeded the truncation engine (centre) selects a strategy and produces a reduced context (right) that is sent to the model.

attrs.alt: Truncation pipeline: full history buffer → truncation engine → reduced context sent to model

content.text: Three elements are always pinned—never subject to truncation—regardless of strategy: the system prompt, the latest user message, and any unmatched tool_call/tool_result pairs from the most recent tool invocation. Everything else is fair game.
Truncation is relevant any time a conversation may exceed the model's context window. Concretely, apply a truncation strategy when:
The agent is expected to run for more than 10–15 turns in a session.
Tool results can be large (search results, database queries, file reads).
The system prompt exceeds 2,000 tokens, leaving less headroom for history.
Input token costs are significant (e.g. GPT-4o at $5/M tokens, Claude at $3/M).
The model's output quality must remain consistent across the full session.
Short, stateless tasks (single-turn Q&A, document summarisation) have no history to truncate.
earlier turns are equally relevant—prefer summarisation over dropping.
Evaluation or audit pipelines where the full transcript must be auditable—store externally instead.
Implementation has four steps: (1) count tokens before each call, (2) check against the budget, (3) apply the selected strategy, and (4) validate that tool pairs remain intact.
— use the model-specific tokenizer (tiktoken for OpenAI, tokenizers for Anthropic's claude) to get an exact token count. Never estimate with character/4.
— total tokens / (window size - max_output_tokens). Trigger truncation when utilisation > 0.70.
— run the selected algorithm. Validate that no tool_call message exists without its partner tool_result and vice-versa.
— emit a metric each time truncation fires. Track which messages were dropped; alert if the system prompt or latest user turn is ever at risk.

import tiktoken

# pip install tiktoken anthropic
ENCODER = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")   # GPT-4 / GPT-4o tokenizer

def count_tokens(messages: list[dict]) -> int:
    """Approximate token count for an OpenAI-format message list."""
    total = 0
    for msg in messages:
        # Each message has ~4 overhead tokens for role + framing
        total += 4
        content = msg.get("content") or ""
        if isinstance(content, list):
            # Multi-modal content blocks (text + image)
            content = " ".join(
                block.get("text", "") for block in content
                if isinstance(block, dict) and block.get("type") == "text"
            )
        total += len(ENCODER.encode(content))
    return total


def build_truncated_context(
    messages: list[dict],
    window_size: int = 128_000,
    max_output_tokens: int = 4_096,
    trigger_ratio: float = 0.70,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return a truncated message list using FIFO-with-pair-safety.
    - messages[0] is assumed to be the system prompt (always kept).
    - The last user message is always kept.
    - Atomic tool_call / tool_result pairs are never split.
    """
    budget = int((window_size - max_output_tokens) * trigger_ratio)
    if count_tokens(messages) <= budget:
        return messages  # no truncation needed

    system = messages[:1]
    tail = messages[-1:]            # latest user turn — always kept
    middle = messages[1:-1]         # everything in between is eligible

    # Build tail from newest-first, stop when we exceed budget
    kept_middle = []
    used = count_tokens(system) + count_tokens(tail)

    i = len(middle) - 1
    while i >= 0:
        msg = middle[i]
        pair = []

        # Collect atomic pair: tool_result must stay with tool_call
        if msg.get("role") == "tool":
            # Find the matching tool_call (previous assistant message)
            if i > 0 and middle[i - 1].get("role") == "assistant":
                pair = [middle[i - 1], msg]
                i -= 2
            else:
                pair = [msg]
                i -= 1
        else:
            pair = [msg]
            i -= 1

        cost = count_tokens(pair)
        if used + cost > budget:
            break   # dropping from here backwards
        kept_middle = pair + kept_middle
        used += cost

    return system + kept_middle + tail
A minimal but correct FIFO implementation that preserves tool pairs and handles the edge case where even the system prompt plus the latest user turn alone exceed the budget.

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")


def tok(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text or ""))


def fifo_truncate(messages: list[dict], max_tokens: int) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Remove oldest user/assistant pairs until the list fits in max_tokens.
    Always preserves messages[0] (system) and messages[-1] (latest user).
    Tool_call + tool_result pairs are removed atomically.
    """
    if not messages:
        return messages

    total = sum(4 + tok(m.get("content") or "") for m in messages)
    if total <= max_tokens:
        return messages

    # Separate pinned messages from the eligible middle
    pinned_head = messages[:1]                  # system prompt
    pinned_tail = messages[-1:]                 # latest user message
    pool = list(messages[1:-1])                 # eligible for removal

    pinned_cost = sum(4 + tok(m.get("content") or "") for m in pinned_head + pinned_tail)
    available = max_tokens - pinned_cost

    # Walk from the end, collecting what fits
    kept = []
    budget_left = available
    i = len(pool) - 1
    while i >= 0:
        if pool[i].get("role") == "tool" and i > 0:
            pair = [pool[i - 1], pool[i]]
            cost = sum(4 + tok(m.get("content") or "") for m in pair)
            if budget_left >= cost:
                kept = pair + kept
                budget_left -= cost
            i -= 2
        else:
            msg = pool[i]
            cost = 4 + tok(msg.get("content") or "")
            if budget_left >= cost:
                kept = [msg] + kept
                budget_left -= cost
            i -= 1

    return pinned_head + kept + pinned_tail


# ── Demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    history = [
        {"role": "system",    "content": "You are a helpful coding assistant."},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "Explain the SOLID principles in detail." * 50},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Sure! SOLID stands for..." + " detail" * 200},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "Now give me a Python example for each." * 30},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Here are examples:" + " code" * 300},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "What is the difference between == and is?"},
    ]
    result = fifo_truncate(history, max_tokens=1_500)
    print(f"Original: {len(history)} messages")
    print(f"Truncated: {len(result)} messages")
    print("Roles kept:", [m["role"] for m in result])
A production-ready class that keeps a running summary of dropped turns and injects it as a synthetic user message so the model retains semantic continuity.

import os
import json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()


class SummarizingContextManager:
    """
    Maintains a message list with automatic summarisation when token usage
    exceeds a configured threshold.

    Usage:
        mgr = SummarizingContextManager(system="You are a helpful assistant.")
        mgr.add_user("Hello! I am Alice, the project lead for Falcon.")
        reply = mgr.call_model()
        mgr.add_assistant(reply)
        ...
    """

    SUMMARISE_PROMPT = (
        "Summarise the following conversation segment in 3-5 sentences. "
        "Preserve: participant names, entity IDs, decisions made, numeric values, "
        "and any open action items. Be concise but complete.\n\n{history}"
    )

    def __init__(
        self,
        system: str,
        model: str = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        window: int = 200_000,
        max_output: int = 8_192,
        trigger_ratio: float = 0.70,
    ):
        self.model = model
        self.window = window
        self.max_output = max_output
        self.trigger_ratio = trigger_ratio
        self.system = system
        self.messages: list[dict] = []
        self.running_summary: str = ""

    def _budget(self) -> int:
        return int((self.window - self.max_output) * self.trigger_ratio)

    def _token_count(self) -> int:
        # Rough estimate: 1 token ≈ 4 chars (fast, good enough for triggering)
        total = len(self.system) // 4
        if self.running_summary:
            total += len(self.running_summary) // 4
        for m in self.messages:
            total += len(str(m.get("content") or "")) // 4
        return total

    def _maybe_summarise(self):
        if self._token_count() <= self._budget():
            return

        # Take the oldest half of the middle messages to summarise
        eligible = self.messages[:-4] if len(self.messages) > 4 else []
        if not eligible:
            return

        history_text = "\n".join(
            f"{m['role'].upper()}: {m.get('content', '')[:500]}"
            for m in eligible
        )
        summary_resp = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=512,
            system="You are a summarisation assistant.",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": self.SUMMARISE_PROMPT.format(history=history_text)}],
        )
        new_summary = summary_resp.content[0].text

        if self.running_summary:
            self.running_summary = f"{self.running_summary}\n\n{new_summary}"
        else:
            self.running_summary = new_summary

        self.messages = self.messages[len(eligible):]

    def add_user(self, content: str):
        self.messages.append({"role": "user", "content": content})
        self._maybe_summarise()

    def add_assistant(self, content: str):
        self.messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": content})

    def build_api_messages(self) -> list[dict]:
        msgs = list(self.messages)
        if self.running_summary:
            # Inject summary as an early user turn
            summary_msg = {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"[Session summary so far]\n{self.running_summary}",
            }
            msgs = [summary_msg] + msgs
        return msgs

    def call_model(self) -> str:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=self.max_output,
            system=self.system,
            messages=self.build_api_messages(),
        )
        return resp.content[0].text


# Quick smoke test (requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)
if __name__ == "__main__" and os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"):
    mgr = SummarizingContextManager(
        system="You are a senior Python engineer.",
        window=200_000,
        max_output=1_024,
        trigger_ratio=0.60,
    )
    mgr.add_user("My name is Alice. I am migrating our Flask app to FastAPI.")
    reply = mgr.call_model()
    mgr.add_assistant(reply)
    print("Summary state:", repr(mgr.running_summary[:100]))
    print("Messages in context:", len(mgr.build_api_messages()))

A customer-support automation pipeline processes 500 concurrent long-lived conversations, each averaging 40 turns. The company uses Claude claude-3-5-haiku-20241022 with a 200 k-token window but needs to keep per-turn costs under $0.005.

Their strategy is a three-layer cascade: (1) tool-result pruning fires immediately after every tool call to strip verbose fields from search results, keeping only title, URL, and a 150-word excerpt; (2) FIFO drop removes the oldest user/assistant pairs when utilisation exceeds 60%; (3) summarisation fires at 75% to compress the retained pairs into a session brief. The session brief is stored in Redis and injected at the top of every subsequent call.

# Abbreviated production-style pipeline used by the support bot
import re

def prune_search_result(raw: str, max_excerpt_words: int = 150) -> str:
    """Strip verbose HTML and truncate the body of a web search result."""
    # Remove HTML tags
    clean = re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", " ", raw)
    # Collapse whitespace
    clean = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", clean).strip()
    # Truncate to max_excerpt_words
    words = clean.split()
    if len(words) > max_excerpt_words:
        clean = " ".join(words[:max_excerpt_words]) + " […]"
    return clean


def prune_tool_results(messages: list[dict], max_words: int = 150) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Walk the message list and truncate any tool_result content that is
    longer than max_words words.
    """
    pruned = []
    for msg in messages:
        if msg.get("role") == "tool":
            content = msg.get("content") or ""
            word_count = len(content.split())
            if word_count > max_words:
                content = prune_search_result(content, max_words)
                msg = {**msg, "content": content}
        pruned.append(msg)
    return pruned


# Cascade: prune → FIFO → summarise (summarise call omitted for brevity)
def prepare_context(messages, budget=120_000):
    messages = prune_tool_results(messages)          # layer 1: prune
    messages = fifo_truncate(messages, budget)        # layer 2: FIFO
    # layer 3: summarise happens in SummarizingContextManager.add_user()
    return messages

In load testing at 500 rps the pipeline adds 12 ms of average latency (tool pruning is synchronous; summarisation is only triggered roughly once per 20 turns). The 75% trigger threshold keeps the summarisation call rare enough that it does not dominate the turn budget.

Removing a tool_result message while keeping the assistant message that contains the matching tool_call (or vice-versa) causes a validation error on OpenAI, Anthropic, and most other APIs. The fix is always to remove both together as an atomic unit.
Waiting for a '400 context_length_exceeded' error means the turn's response is already lost and the agent must retry. Proactive truncation at 70–80% utilisation avoids this. Monitor the
field in the API response to track real utilisation.

Some naïve implementations sort messages by length and drop the largest one first. If the system prompt is long (common in production with tool schemas embedded), this algorithm will evict it. The system prompt must be unconditionally pinned at position 0.

A summary that says 'the user mentioned a database' instead of 'the user mentioned PostgreSQL 16 with the pgvector extension hosted on AWS RDS in eu-west-1' loses the specificity the model needs to give useful answers later. The summarisation prompt must explicitly instruct the model to preserve named entities, IDs, numbers, and dates.
: the system prompt, the most recent user message, and any open tool_call/tool_result pair.
, not at 100%. Leaving 20–30% headroom accommodates verbose model outputs and avoids edge cases where the truncated list itself marginally exceeds budget.
before dropping whole messages. A 5,000-token tool result can often be trimmed to 300 tokens with no loss of reasoning-relevant content.
(database or object store). The model sees the truncated view; humans and audit logs see everything.
. Unit tests that only check 5-turn conversations will miss failures that occur at turn 40 when the first summarisation fires.
: truncations per session, tokens dropped per turn, summarisation trigger count. Alert when truncation fires on turn 3 (indicates the system prompt is too large).
Validate with the model's own tokenizer
. Character-count heuristics can be off by ±20%. Use tiktoken (OpenAI) or the anthropic client's built-in token counter.

The computational cost of token counting is O(n) in message length. For a typical 50-turn session with 200 tokens per turn the counting step takes under 5 ms on CPU—negligible compared to network latency. However, re-counting the full history on every turn is wasteful; the incremental approach is:

Summarisation adds latency proportional to the summarised segment length. A 30-turn segment produces a summary in roughly 300–600 ms on a fast model. To hide this latency, trigger summarisation asynchronously after a turn completes rather than blocking the next call. The summary is ready well before the next turn's 70% threshold is reached.

At scale (thousands of concurrent sessions) the summarisation call becomes the primary bottleneck. Mitigation strategies include: caching summaries keyed by message-list hash, using a smaller summarisation model, and throttling summarisation calls with a semaphore to avoid thundering-herd effects when many sessions hit 70% simultaneously.
— exact counts for GPT-4, GPT-4o, and o-series models. pip install tiktoken.
returns the exact prompt token count before making an API call.
— supports any model with a published tokenizer.json; works for Llama 3, Mistral, etc.

# LangChain — ConversationTokenBufferMemory does FIFO truncation automatically
from langchain.memory import ConversationTokenBufferMemory
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")
memory = ConversationTokenBufferMemory(
    llm=llm,
    max_token_limit=4_000,
    return_messages=True,
)
# Memory automatically drops oldest turns to stay within max_token_limit

# LangChain — ConversationSummaryBufferMemory does summarise+FIFO
from langchain.memory import ConversationSummaryBufferMemory
memory_s = ConversationSummaryBufferMemory(
    llm=llm,
    max_token_limit=4_000,
    return_messages=True,
)
# When limit is exceeded it summarises older turns and keeps the summary
Truncation is one of several context-management strategies. The table below compares the main approaches across the dimensions that matter most in production.
Chat apps where recency is all that matters

A malicious payload injected early in a session (e.g. hidden in a retrieved document) can be truncated away before it executes—but it can also survive into a summary if the summarisation model faithfully reproduces it. Sanitise tool results before they enter the message list, not just before they are displayed.

If multiple users share a single long-lived agent session (multi-tenant agent), their messages may be co-mingled in a summary. Always scope the history and any summaries to a single user session. Use session IDs to partition storage.
Use a high-confidence model (not the cheapest one) for summarisation.
Provide a structured output schema so the summary follows a fixed template with named fields.
After generating the summary, optionally re-read it in the same turn and ask the model to flag any factual inconsistencies.
Store the original messages in a separate audit log so errors can be detected post-hoc.
If the summarisation call itself fails (network error, rate limit), the agent should fall back to FIFO drop rather than crashing. Always wrap summarisation in a try/except with a fallback path.
Context truncation is a mandatory concern for any agent expected to run beyond 10–15 turns; ignoring it leads to silent degradation or hard API errors.
Three messages are always pinned and never truncatable: the system prompt, the latest user turn, and any unmatched tool_call/tool_result pair.
Trigger truncation proactively at 70–80% token utilisation, not reactively on API failure. Use the model's own tokenizer for accurate counts.
Prune verbose tool results first; they are often the largest single item in the context and can be compressed by 10–20× with minimal loss.
Summarise-and-replace preserves semantic continuity at the cost of one extra LLM call per trigger event; protect against summarisation hallucinations with structured prompts and audit logs.
Persist the full, untruncated history externally (database or object store); the model sees only the truncated view while humans and audits see everything.
Hybrid strategies—tool pruning, then FIFO, then summarisation—give the best balance of information retention, latency, and cost for production long-session agents.
Emit metrics for every truncation event (tokens dropped, strategy triggered, turn number) and alert on anomalies; truncation behaviour is a leading indicator of agent health.
